reasoning: The core speculative decoding pipeline is well-specified and internally consistent: draft model training via distillation, batched verification with branch-aware attention masks, and the standard acceptance-rejection correction are all established, compatible mechanisms. The adaptive K/B sizing via a "tiny L probe" introduces a mild tension — using L's one-step logits as a probe partially defeats the purpose of avoiding L passes, though the method acknowledges this as a lightweight check and it is not a blocking flaw. The multi-step prediction head and tree-of-proposals are described at a level where an implementer could make reasonable design choices to fill gaps. The online calibration layer and microbatching across requests are standard engineering additions that don't conflict with the core method. No fundamental contradictions exist between the stated components.

The method is broadly coherent and combines well-established components (speculative decoding, KV caching, ANN retrieval, distillation, online RL-style updates) in a compatible way. The core speculative decoding loop — draft with S, verify with L, accept/reject with exact probability correction — is standard and correctly described. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the "merge and rank candidate runs under S" step from heterogeneous sources (beam expansion + retrieval) is under-specified — how exactly token-run probabilities from the retriever are scored under S's distribution is not detailed; (2) the batch verification with "select the longest fully verified run" mixes deterministic and sampling acceptance in a way that could subtly break the exact distribution guarantee unless carefully handled (the method doesn't address this); (3) the online verifier-in-the-loop update with a KL regularizer is mentioned but the training loop mechanics (when updates fire, how cached traffic is collected, gradient flow) are vague enough that an implementer would need to make significant design choices. These are notable gaps but not fundamental contradictions — the overall pipeline is implementable with reasonable engineering decisions filling in the blanks.

The method is a well-specified extension of standard speculative decoding (Leviathan et al., Chen et al.) with clearly described components: a parallel draft model using two-stream masked attention, a single batched verification pass reusing KV-cache, and exact acceptance-correction. The acceptance-correction procedure is correctly described and is a known valid technique for preserving the target distribution. The main coherence concern is the two-stream position-specific masked attention architecture for D — while inspired by XLNet-style two-stream attention, the exact masking scheme needed to predict B tokens simultaneously without any autoregressive dependency among them (yet conditioned on the prefix) requires careful specification that is somewhat underspecified here, but not fatally so. The multi-hypothesis head selection logic ("selecting the first block that fully accepts the longest prefix") is slightly ambiguous but not blocking. Overall, an ML practitioner familiar with speculative decoding and transformer attention variants could implement this with reasonable design choices.

The method is a well-structured extension of speculative decoding with tree/trie verification, depth-specialized heads, and an uncertainty-guided pruning mechanism. The core speculative sampling acceptance-correction rule is standard and correctly stated. The main coherence concerns are: (1) the "depth-specialized heads" on D require the shared backbone to produce representations useful for multi-step lookahead, but training these heads via KL on teacher-forced continuations is a reasonable and implementable procedure; (2) the uncertainty head is trained to predict per-candidate acceptance likelihood using L's responses as pseudo-targets, which is coherent in principle but the exact supervision signal construction (how acceptance probability targets are derived without running full inference loops at training time) is somewhat underspecified; (3) the trie-packing with KV cache reuse for shared prefixes is a known technique and compatible with the rest of the method. Overall the pieces fit together without fundamental contradictions, and an ML practitioner familiar with speculative decoding could implement this with reasonable design choices to fill minor gaps in the uncertainty head training and trie-batching details.

The method is broadly coherent and well-specified. The pipeline flows logically: encode once, score tokens, merge low-importance ones via agglomerative clustering, pool KV matrices, and decode over reduced tokens. The distortion objective in KV space is well-defined and the pooling matrix construction is clear. One notable tension is the "text-only forward pass through the first L_p decoder layers to obtain query vectors Q_l" — in most VLM architectures, the decoder's cross-attention queries depend on both text and visual context interleaved, so obtaining clean text-only queries that are meaningful for visual token saliency requires the architecture to support a clean text-only prefix pass, which is not universally the case and would need architectural-specific handling. The elastic refinement step (replacing visual KV mid-generation) is feasible but the mechanism for "regenerating from the next step" while retaining text KV caches needs careful implementation detail around positional consistency. These are real but not blocking issues — an ML practitioner could make reasonable design choices to handle them.

The method is broadly coherent and combines well-established components (attention-based token scoring, k-means++ clustering, cosine similarity redundancy, token merging, RoPE offset adjustment) in a compatible way. The main coherence weaknesses are in the progressive decoding phase: the "unmerge and admit next tranche" step is internally contradictory — tokens were merged into kept neighbors (their embeddings pooled away), so there is no clean way to "unmerge" them without storing the original embeddings separately, which is not explicitly stated as a requirement. Additionally, the mechanism of "updating KV entries only in later layers" for newly admitted tokens is under-specified — it is unclear how partial-layer KV updates are implemented in standard transformer inference without a full forward pass or significant architectural surgery. The PDM-style masking of vision contributions mid-generation also requires non-trivial KV-cache manipulation that is described only at a high level. These are notable gaps an implementer would need to redesign, but the core static token selection and merging pipeline is coherent and implementable.

The method is broadly coherent and well-specified across its three stages. The saliency scoring, clustering, and merging steps are all grounded in standard ML primitives (attention weights, ANN indexing, weighted averaging, SVD). The rescaling heuristic (dividing by sqrt(sum w_i^2)) is a reasonable approximation but is not rigorously derived — it assumes independence and linearity that may not hold in softmax attention, and the "attention decoupling principles" cited are vague. The adaptive split mechanism is logically consistent but the triggering condition mixes two criteria (intra-cluster variance and distribution deviation from full-cache baseline) without specifying how they are combined or thresholded, leaving an implementer to make non-trivial design choices. The "ghost token" placeholder mechanism for fixed-length VLMs is a reasonable patch but introduces a subtle inconsistency: zeroed-value tokens still participate in softmax normalization and could distort attention distributions unless masked, which is not addressed. These are notable but not blocking gaps — an experienced ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable choices.

The method is broadly coherent and well-specified. The three scoring signals are clearly defined and combinable, the pruning/merging pipeline follows a logical sequence, and the plug-and-play constraint (no encoder retraining) is consistently respected throughout. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the two-pass pseudo-relevance feedback requires running a partial forward pass through the language model's cross-attention to extract "top attended visual tokens," which means the LM must be partially executed before the token set is finalized — this creates a non-trivial implementation dependency that is under-specified (e.g., how exactly to cache and reuse text-side states, how to re-inject the revised token set mid-inference); (2) preserving positional statistics for rotary/relative encodings via "averaging angles or keeping the dominant orientation" is vague and may not be straightforwardly compatible with all positional encoding schemes without additional design work; (3) the adaptive EMA blending of three heterogeneous signals lacks a concrete initialization or normalization scheme, leaving the implementer to make non-trivial design choices. These are notable gaps but not fundamental contradictions — a skilled ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable engineering decisions.

The method is broadly coherent and well-specified, with a clear two-phase training procedure, defined controller inputs/outputs, and concrete refinement options. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the "micro-steps" mechanism assumes the base model can be decomposed into reusable sub-block passes that share KV-cache state, which is non-trivial for standard transformer architectures and requires significant architectural modification that is underspecified; (2) the three-way refinement branch (deeper steps, wider MoE experts, deliberation pass) requires the controller to make a discrete choice among options under a budget, but the mechanism for this joint selection is not specified beyond "the controller chooses among them"; (3) the verifier for deliberation scoring is described as "compact" but its training procedure and integration are not detailed. These are notable gaps an implementer would need to fill, but none are fundamental contradictions — a practitioner could make reasonable design choices to resolve them.

ACD is a well-structured proposal with mostly compatible components, but it has several notable coherence gaps. The training procedure requires simultaneously learning a process reward model, a value head ψ_t, and an FFN-gating predictor from self-sampled trajectories, but the interdependencies and training order/stability of these three learned components are underspecified — particularly how ψ_t bootstraps before the process reward model is calibrated, and how the dense-retrieval datastore interacts with the base model's hidden states at inference time without architectural changes. The "dense-retrieval proposals keyed by the model's current hidden state" in the Easy regime implies an offline datastore of hidden-state-to-token-sequence mappings, which is a non-trivial infrastructure requirement that is only briefly mentioned as built "offline" without specifying what corpus or how it generalizes. The routing thresholds τ_easy and τ_hard are tuned on a validation set, but the runtime adaptive budget-shifting mechanism (monitoring acceptance rates to shift budgets within a latency envelope) introduces a feedback loop whose stability and interaction with the fixed thresholds is unexplained. These are real gaps an implementer would need to redesign, but none individually constitutes a fundamental contradiction — the overall paradigm is coherent and the fallback guarantee is logically sound.

The method combines several well-established components (early exits, saturation signals, masked attention, speculative/draft decoding, Picard iteration) in a layered pipeline that is broadly compatible. Each stage has a defined fallback, and the training auxiliaries map onto the inference-time decisions. However, there are notable coherence gaps: the "masked refinement loop" using a compact residual block that re-attends to only top-k context tokens is underspecified — it is unclear how this residual block integrates with the transformer's KV cache and whether it shares weights or is a separate module, requiring non-trivial design choices. The Picard-style sweep conditioning on "cached tokens elsewhere" while selectively re-evaluating subsets creates potential consistency issues (updated tokens affect cached states of neighbors) that are not addressed. The interaction between the chunkwise verification and the masked refinement loop — both triggered by low confidence — is not clearly delineated, leaving ambiguity about which mechanism takes precedence and how they compose. These are notable but not fatal gaps; an experienced ML practitioner could fill them in with reasonable design choices, but would need to make several non-trivial architectural decisions not specified in the method.

TAP is broadly coherent and combines recognizable components (difficulty head, Kalman smoothing, refinement loop, no-regret guard) in compatible ways. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the "no-regret guard" relies on a verifier score to compare the refined token against the baseline, but the verifier is only loosely specified (distilled from a stronger model or task metric proxy) and its applicability at the single-token level during autoregressive decoding is under-specified — token-level verifier scores are non-trivial to define and the training signal for this is not clearly grounded; (2) the two refinement modes (depth-on-demand recurrent block vs. candidate self-consistency) are described as interchangeable but have fundamentally different computational graphs and training requirements, and how they share the pairwise preference scorer is not fully reconciled; (3) batching tokens by k_t across a batch is mentioned as a throughput optimization but the mechanics of doing this within standard autoregressive decoding (where positions are sequential) would require non-trivial engineering that is glossed over. These are notable gaps an implementer would need to fill, but none are outright contradictions that block implementation entirely.

The method is broadly coherent and describes a plausible three-tier KV cache system with well-specified components (low-rank projection, product quantization, IVF-PQ indexing, Gumbel-Softmax routing, straight-through estimators). The main coherence tensions are: (1) the "mixture-of-memory kernel" combining logits from exact, dequantized, and summary-derived attention with log-sum-exp normalization is described at a high level but the precise mechanism for making these logits commensurable (they come from different key spaces — projected vs. original vs. centroid) is underspecified and would require non-trivial design choices; (2) the back-pointer promotion mechanism ("reintroduced on demand") implies storing evicted tokens somewhere retrievable, but the method doesn't clarify whether evicted Tier-0 tokens are retained in Tier-1 or truly evicted, creating a mild inconsistency; (3) the differentiable simulation of three tiers during continued pretraining is feasible in principle but the interaction between the Gumbel-Softmax tier selection and the IVF-PQ indexing (which is non-differentiable at inference) creates a train-inference gap that is acknowledged only implicitly. These are notable gaps but not fundamental blockers — an experienced ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable design choices.

CASCADE-KV is a detailed and largely coherent system combining tiered KV storage, importance prediction, diversity-aware eviction, compression, and on-demand restoration. The individual components (KMeans clustering, product quantization, random projections, attention-based scoring) are all well-understood and compatible. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the importance predictor that forecasts future attention over the next W steps is described as "lightweight" but its training procedure (from attention traces with cost-regularized objective) is only vaguely specified — an implementer would need to design the architecture and loss in detail; (2) the two-stage attention with on-demand far-store fetch introduces a mid-forward-pass asynchronous memory operation that requires non-trivial runtime infrastructure (interrupting the attention kernel, fetching from CPU/SSD, recomputing partial attention) that is acknowledged but not specified in enough detail to be directly implementable; (3) the interaction between the per-head precision allocation, the cluster proxy mechanism, and the controller's dynamic budget adjustment creates multiple interacting feedback loops whose joint training and inference-time coordination is underspecified. These are notable gaps but not fundamental contradictions — a skilled ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable engineering choices.

The method is broadly coherent and combines recognizable components (tiered caching, learned predictors, span summarization, distillation-based adaptation) in compatible ways. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the span viewer tokens require running the frozen attention stack over each span at demotion time, but the mechanism for doing this mid-decode without disrupting the autoregressive generation loop is underspecified — it's unclear when exactly these viewer tokens are computed and whether positional encodings remain consistent; (2) the cache-dropout training procedure assumes access to "long-context traces from the target model," which is a non-trivial prerequisite that may require significant infrastructure and is not fully specified in terms of how the student's positional indices are handled when tokens are zeroed out; (3) the interaction between the three-tier system and batched inference (where different sequences may have different retention schedules) adds implementation complexity that is glossed over. These are notable gaps but not fundamental contradictions — an experienced ML practitioner could make reasonable design choices to fill them without redesigning the core approach.

The method is broadly coherent and combines recognizable components (ring buffer exact window, prototype/clustering compression, Kalman-style utility tracking, lazy rehydration from CPU buffers) in compatible ways. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the "lazy rehydration" step assumes original KV vectors are stored somewhere (CPU-pinned buffers or compact residuals) but this storage mechanism is only vaguely specified — it's unclear whether full KVs are always retained on CPU or only residuals, and how residuals are reconstructed without the full KV; (2) the asynchronous rehydration affecting "the next few decoding steps" introduces a temporal mismatch that is not fully resolved — the exact synchronization protocol between async fetch and the attention computation pipeline is underspecified; (3) the two-stage training requires logging full-attention runs and training a ranker via distillation, which is a non-trivial prerequisite that interacts with the online Kalman calibration in ways not fully reconciled. These are notable gaps but not fundamental contradictions — an ML practitioner could make reasonable design choices to fill them without redesigning the core approach.

The pipeline is broadly coherent and well-specified across its four stages, with compatible mechanisms throughout. The most notable coherence weakness is the inference-time precision router: dynamically elevating activation precision mid-sequence requires on-the-fly dequantization and requantization of activations and KV caches, which is technically non-trivial and the method leaves the hardware/kernel implementation underspecified (e.g., how mixed-precision activations coexist in a single forward pass without custom CUDA kernels or significant overhead). The bilevel optimization loop (outer discrete bit-width selector + inner QAT) is described at a high level but the interaction between Gumbel-soft selectors and the discrete hardware-friendly group-wise schemes creates a tension that would require non-trivial engineering to resolve. The MRF smoothing of importance signals and the probe training are reasonable additions but add implementation complexity without full specification. These are real gaps but none are fundamental contradictions — an experienced ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable design choices.

The method is broadly coherent and describes a plausible pipeline with well-defined components: offline importance scoring via attention mixing ratios, activation patching, and Fisher diagonals; mixed-precision weight assignment; per-channel refinement folded into weights; a tiny online predictor for activation tiers; and a QAT fine-tuning pass. These components are individually established techniques combined in a compatible way. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the online predictor that predicts which FFN neurons will be active before the FFN runs creates a mild chicken-and-egg timing issue that requires careful architectural placement but is not insurmountable; (2) the "two activation tiers per layer" with dynamic masking requires custom CUDA kernels that are non-trivial to implement on commodity runtimes, and the claim of engine compatibility is somewhat optimistic given the dynamic nature of the tier masks; (3) the feedback loop that re-runs counterfactual patching at deployment time to adjust precision is described as a deployment-time procedure but relies on the same offline causal patching infrastructure, which is underspecified for online use. These are notable gaps an implementer would need to fill but do not constitute fundamental contradictions.

RAMP-Q is a detailed, multi-stage method with generally compatible components. The attribution stage (GradCAM-style relevance + ablation deltas), mixed-precision assignment, activation-outlier regularization via per-channel α factors, and reasoning-focused distillation all fit together without paradigm conflicts. The runtime scheduler is a plausible add-on. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the per-channel refinement factor α "folded into weights" for activation quantization is described at a level of abstraction that leaves the exact folding mechanics underspecified — an implementer would need to make non-trivial design choices about how this interacts with group-wise quantization and engine compatibility; (2) the "randomized quantized substitutions during training" step mixes straight-through estimator-style training with the distillation objective in a way that is not fully specified (e.g., how gradients flow through the substituted activations, whether a separate STE is used); (3) the two-pass stabilization with embedding/state replacement is vague about what "replace their embeddings/states" means mechanically. These are real gaps but none is a fundamental contradiction — an experienced ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable choices without redesigning the overall approach.

APQ is a detailed, multi-stage pipeline with generally compatible components. The core idea — criticality mapping, mixed-precision allocation, QAT with alignment losses, and runtime precision elevation — is internally consistent and each stage feeds plausibly into the next. However, there are notable coherence gaps. The runtime selector that "detects core reasoning steps on-the-fly" is described as using local features (token surprisal, attention entropy) and being O(1) per token, but the mechanism for dynamically switching activation precision mid-forward-pass is not specified — modern hardware (e.g., tensor cores) does not trivially support per-token precision switching for activations and KV cache without significant kernel-level engineering that is left entirely unaddressed. The "prototype-based head" for discriminative uncertainty is mentioned but its training procedure and integration into the criticality map are underspecified. The scaffolded bit-dropping schedule's stopping criterion ("within tolerance") is vague. These are real implementation gaps an ML practitioner would need to redesign, particularly the dynamic activation precision switching at inference, which is a non-trivial systems challenge that could block the approach as described.

The method is broadly coherent and combines well-established components (LoRA-style adapters, 4-bit quantization, MoE-style routing, KD, progressive rank growth) in a compatible way. The most notable coherence weakness is the interaction between shared adapter experts and progressive rank growth: when experts are shared across all layers and ranks are dynamically expanded/pruned based on per-layer gradient sketches, it is unclear how per-layer gradient signals map back to shared expert ranks — expanding a shared expert's rank based on one layer's gradient sketch affects all layers using that expert, creating a conflict between the "per-layer" rank allocation logic and the "shared across layers" expert design. Additionally, the optional prefix bank being "also selected by the router" introduces a mild under-specification (the router must now jointly select from two different adapter types with different interfaces). These are notable gaps an implementer would need to resolve by design, but they do not fundamentally block implementation of the core pipeline.

The method combines several well-established components (LoRA, zeroth-order optimization, soft prompts, sensitivity-based rank allocation) in a broadly compatible way. The core pipeline is coherent: sensitivity scan → rank allocation → ZO training with LoRA adapters → optional soft prompt warm-up. However, there are notable under-specifications that an implementer would need to resolve: the "distillation" of soft prompt effects into adapters via "regressing early hidden-state shifts" is vague and lacks a concrete loss formulation; the "bidirectional KL calibration" for the DropKey/HiddenCut train-inference gap is not explained mechanistically; and the knowledge adapter's "encoder-projector" architecture and how it interfaces with the ZO training loop is left undefined. These are not fatal contradictions but represent genuine design gaps requiring non-trivial decisions. The ZO estimator applied to LoRA parameters is coherent in principle, though convergence with very low-rank adapters under ZO noise is a known practical challenge that the method acknowledges only superficially.

The method combines several well-established components (hypernetworks, LoRA-style adapters, attention prefixes, zeroth-order optimization) in a broadly compatible way. The main coherence tension is between the hypernetwork generating variable-rank adapter weights on-the-fly and the zeroth-order optimization scheme: ZO estimators are known to scale poorly with parameter count, and while the hypernetwork is small, the method claims to support dynamic rank allocation where the hypernetwork must emit variable-shaped weight tensors — the "factored weights with shared basis plus per-layer coefficients" mechanism is described but under-specified enough that an implementer would need to make non-trivial design choices about how the hypernetwork output dimensionality changes as ranks are reallocated mid-training. Additionally, the curvature-aware rank/prefix budgeting relies on "zeroth-order gradient variance" as a proxy for Hessian curvature, which is a reasonable heuristic but the interaction between dynamic rank changes and the hypernetwork's fixed output head is not fully resolved. These are notable gaps but not fundamental contradictions; the core loop (freeze LLM, train small hypernetwork with ZO, generate adapters/prefixes) is implementable, and the optional distillation and layer-norm unfreezing are standard additions.

The method combines well-established components (LoRA, prefix tuning, multiplicative gates, DPO-style loss, RAG) in a broadly compatible way, and an ML practitioner could implement most of it using existing libraries. The most notable coherence weaknesses are: (1) the "loss-adaptive optimizer" with per-module EMA-based learning rate and momentum adjustment is described at a high level without specifying how per-module noise estimates are computed or how they interact with standard optimizers like AdamW — an implementer would need to design this from scratch; (2) the uncertainty-weighted update scheme that blends "fast updates with an EMA path" for prefix/gate modules is under-specified in terms of how the two gradient paths are combined and when the EMA is applied relative to the optimizer step; (3) pseudo-relevance feedback during training (using the model's own retrieved passages) introduces a potential training-inference inconsistency that is acknowledged but not resolved mechanistically. These gaps are real but not fatal — each could be filled with reasonable engineering choices without redesigning the overall framework.

The method is broadly coherent and combines well-established components (test-time adaptation, contrastive learning, EMA teacher-student, Sinkhorn balancing, feature whitening) in a compatible way. The core pipeline — frozen text encoder as anchor, visual adapters updated via unsupervised losses, bridge tokens shared across modalities — is internally consistent and implementable. The main coherence weakness is the "bridge tokens shared by both modalities" mechanism: the text encoder is stated to be frozen, yet bridge tokens are prepended to text and updated during adaptation, which creates a mild tension (updating inputs to a frozen encoder is feasible but the interaction with the frozen text encoder's output space needs care). Additionally, the stopping criterion based on entropy plateau is under-specified (no concrete threshold or window defined), and the feature whitening toward "stored source statistics" assumes those statistics are available at deployment, which is not explicitly addressed in the setup. These are fillable gaps rather than blocking contradictions, and an ML practitioner could resolve them with reasonable design choices.

The method is broadly coherent and describes a plausible unsupervised domain adaptation pipeline for vision-language models. The three stages are logically sequenced and compatible: textual inversion for the domain token, contrastive fine-tuning with pseudo-captions and hard negatives, and lightweight adapter modules are all established techniques that can be combined. However, there are notable gaps an implementer would need to resolve: the "target-style remover" for generating style-stripped captions is mentioned but not specified (what model or procedure achieves this?); the "modality contribution score" is introduced as a key stabilization mechanism but its computation is never defined; the "Bridger modules" enabling bidirectional attention between frozen encoders are described only abstractly without specifying architecture or how they interface with frozen backbones; and the "compute-aware scaling laws" for source data mixing are referenced without any concrete formulation. These are not fatal contradictions but represent multiple under-specified components that would require non-trivial design decisions by an implementer.

The method is elaborate but broadly coherent. Each of the three main ingredients (mixed-encoder visual adaptor, preference mining, self-distillation with replay) is described with sufficient specificity that an ML practitioner could implement them using existing components (DINOv2, CLIP, Q-Former, DPO loss, EMA teacher). The interactions between components are logically compatible: the saliency maps from DINOv2 attention feed into preference pair construction, the EMA teacher generates pseudo-captions for self-distillation, and source replay prevents forgetting. One notable gap is the "region-neglect pairs" construction: the preferred response y* is generated by the current (adapting) model, but using the model's own output as the preferred label in a DPO-style objective creates a circular dependency where the reference and target distributions are entangled in a non-standard way that could destabilize training. Additionally, the confidence weighting scheme combining perplexity, CLIP margin, and view disagreement is described at a high level without specifying how these signals are normalized or combined, requiring implementer judgment. The optional PixelHop++-style features are mentioned but not integrated into the projection pipeline with enough detail. These are notable but not blocking gaps — a competent practitioner could make reasonable design choices to fill them.

The method is broadly coherent and combines well-established components (visual prompts, adversarial domain adaptation, InfoNCE, teacher-student distillation, retrieval-based pseudo-labeling) in a compatible way. The two-stage training pipeline is clearly sequenced and the inference-time simplification (dropping discriminator, retrieval, teacher) is consistent with the architecture. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the "uncertainty-guided EMA that adapts faster on hard, out-of-domain samples" is somewhat paradoxical — standard EMA-based prompt updates would need careful specification of how uncertainty inverts the update rate, and the interaction with the prototype-based confidence head is under-specified; (2) the variational information bottleneck on z_shared requires a reparameterization scheme and a prior that are not defined, and how this interacts with the deterministic uncertainty head is left vague; (3) the masked-patch recovery conditioned on retrieved texts is mentioned as an auxiliary task but its architecture and loss integration are not specified. These are real gaps an implementer would need to fill in, but none individually blocks the overall approach from being built with reasonable design choices.

The four-stage pipeline is broadly coherent and each stage builds on the previous in a compatible way. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) Stage A's self-supervised objectness training via teacher-student multi-view consistency is well-established, but the "self-training on high-IoU self-consistent proposals" for box regression creates a circular dependency that is under-specified — it is unclear what the initial box targets are before any self-training has converged. (2) Stage D's cluster-to-name assignment via "highest average similarity" assumes the clustering produces semantically clean groupings, but with noisy pseudo-labels from Stage C feeding the memory bank, this assumption is optimistic and the feedback loop between Stage C and Stage D could amplify errors in ways not addressed. (3) The energy model for adaptive thresholding is mentioned but its fitting procedure (what distribution, what data) is only vaguely described, leaving an implementer to make significant design choices. These are notable gaps but not fundamental contradictions; an experienced ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable design decisions without redesigning the overall architecture.

The method is broadly coherent and combines well-established components (DETR-style set prediction, CLIP/DINO frozen encoders, prototype memory banks, EMA updates, Hungarian matching) in a plausible pipeline. The three-loop training scheme is described with enough specificity that an ML practitioner could implement it. However, there are notable gaps: the "Distributional Ranking" objective is named but not defined (no loss formula or reference), the "domain-aware gate" and "domain indicator" are vaguely specified, and the interaction between Loop C's teacher distillation and the online pseudo-label refinement in Loop A creates a potential circular dependency that is not resolved. The alternating optimization between clustering and metric learning is described at a high level but the convergence criteria and scheduling between loops are unspecified. These are not fatal contradictions but would require non-trivial design decisions by an implementer.

The method is broadly coherent and combines well-established components (DETR-style set prediction, contrastive region-text alignment, dynamic heads) in a compatible pipeline. The main coherence weakness is the inference-time clustering step for novel objects: the method states that unaligned regions are clustered "across the dataset" in embedding space to form pseudo-classes, but this requires storing and processing region embeddings from the entire dataset at inference time, which is not fully specified as an online or offline procedure and creates a practical ambiguity about when and how this runs relative to per-image detection. Additionally, the dynamic binary head parameter generation by "fusing text embedding with global image statistics" is described at a level of abstraction that leaves the implementer to design the fusion architecture. These are notable but not blocking gaps — an ML practitioner could make reasonable design choices to fill them without fundamentally redesigning the method.

The method is broadly coherent and combines recognizable components (class-agnostic proposals, contrastive prototype learning, teacher-student self-training, visual prompts, Bradley-Terry ranking) in compatible ways. However, there are several notable under-specifications that an implementer would need to resolve: the "Distinction Maximization" prototype layer is named but not defined mechanistically (how it produces calibrated uncertainty in a single pass is not explained); the partitioning of the latent into shared objectness vs. prototype-specific subspaces with "learned priors per cluster" lacks a concrete formulation; and the uncertainty-aware EMA that "increases update weight on uncertain samples" inverts the typical EMA stability logic in a way that is not reconciled with the stated goal of keeping stable anchors. The interaction between the retrieval-driven pseudo relevance feedback loop and the contrastive loss is described at a high level but the consistency graph construction across iterations is vague enough to require significant design decisions. These are collectively notable gaps rather than fundamental contradictions, placing the method at a 3.

The method is broadly coherent and combines well-established offline RL components (behavior cloning, ensemble Q-functions, pessimism, doubly robust OPE, trust-region updates) in a compatible way. The main coherence weakness is the proximal projection step: "finding the nearest action with sufficient behavior likelihood and low Mahalanobis distance" requires solving a constrained optimization problem in action space at every policy update step, and the mechanism for doing this efficiently (especially in continuous high-dimensional action spaces) is underspecified — the "convex blend with behavior actions" fallback is vague about how the blend weights are computed. Additionally, the closed-loop acceptance gate that simultaneously adjusts pessimism, support threshold, and step size lacks a concrete update rule, leaving an implementer to design the adaptation schedule from scratch. These are notable gaps but not fundamental contradictions; an experienced ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable design choices (e.g., gradient-based projection, heuristic schedules).

The method is broadly coherent and combines well-established components (VQ/K-means quantization, conformal prediction, CQL, ensemble LCB, behavior cloning prior) in a compatible pipeline. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the conformal calibration step conflates two different notions — it calibrates a global threshold κ on nonconformity scores defined relative to codebook representatives, but the coverage guarantee applies to logged (s,a) pairs, not to the codebook actions themselves, creating a subtle mismatch between what is guaranteed and what is actually used at deployment; (2) the CQL-style objective is described as "lowering Q on dataset actions outside the support," but dataset actions are by construction the source of the support, so this clause is somewhat contradictory and would require implementer clarification; (3) the residual head that refines within an ε-ball while staying "inside support" is under-specified — the support is a discrete codebook set, so a continuous ε-ball refinement needs a separate mechanism to verify support membership. These are notable gaps but not fundamental blockers; an experienced ML practitioner could resolve them with reasonable design choices.

The method is broadly coherent and combines well-established offline RL components (support filtering, pessimistic Q-learning, advantage-weighted regression, doubly robust OPE) in a consistent pipeline. The most notable coherence weakness is the calibration of bφ from implicit interventions across multiple loggers: the procedure of identifying (s,a) pairs under different loggers and fitting with weighted cross-entropy to match cross-logger assignment frequencies is described at a high level but leaves the implementer to work out how to identify these pairs, how to compute the weights, and how to handle the case where logger identities are unknown — this is a non-trivial missing specification. Additionally, the sparsemax_α inside the Bellman target mixes a sparse probability operator with a max operation in a way that is slightly ambiguous (is it a soft-max replacement or an expectation?), requiring the implementer to make a design choice. These gaps are real but not blocking — a competent ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable design decisions without fundamentally redesigning the method.

RAPTOR combines several well-established offline RL components (nearest-neighbor retrieval, pessimistic critics, pairwise ranking, model-based rollouts, belief states) in a broadly compatible way. The core pipeline is coherent: retrieve candidates, apply pessimistic value estimation, rank within candidates, and train an actor with behavior regularization. However, there are notable under-specifications that an implementer would need to resolve: (1) the prototype-based discriminative layer for single-pass uncertainty is described vaguely — it's unclear how prototypes are defined, updated, and how the uncertainty head is architecturally integrated with the value network; (2) the joint training of pointwise value signals and pairwise preference model introduces potential gradient conflict that is not addressed; (3) the belief state learning in partially observed domains is mentioned as a component but its training procedure and integration with the retrieval index are left underspecified. These are not fatal contradictions but represent meaningful design gaps requiring non-trivial decisions by the implementer.

The method is broadly coherent and well-specified. The pipeline — dense retrieval, precomputed token-level and per-head document embeddings, context-conditioned gating at query time, and a fusion MLP — fits together without paradigm conflicts. The two-stage training (cross-encoder distillation then calibration fine-tuning) is standard and compatible with the frozen document encoder assumption. The main under-specification is how the H context heads are initialized and trained: the method says they capture "entities, temporal cues, sentiment, discourse roles" but does not explain how these semantically distinct heads are defined or supervised before distillation begins — an implementer would need to make non-trivial design choices here. Product quantization of per-head token embeddings is mentioned but the interaction between quantization fidelity and the gating mechanism is not addressed. These are fillable gaps rather than blocking contradictions.

The overall pipeline is coherent and each stage connects logically: frozen multi-view document embeddings, query-side context enrichment, late-interaction scoring, optional LLM listwise reranking, and score blending. The main coherence weakness is the optional "refinement iteration" step — using a RankNet-style gradient over the retrieved pool at inference time to update the query representation is under-specified (how many gradient steps, what loss surface, how this interacts with the adapter weights vs. a per-query optimization, and whether this is test-time fine-tuning or something else). This step mixes training-time and inference-time paradigms without clarifying the mechanism. Additionally, the "multi-view document embeddings" (k viewer vectors per document) and how they are generated is left vague — the method assumes they exist but doesn't specify how facets are defined or how the viewer vectors are trained, which an implementer would need to design. These are notable gaps but not fatal; the rest of the pipeline (retrieval, scoring formula, LLM reranking, fusion, two-stage training) is well-specified enough that a practitioner could implement the core system with reasonable design choices.

The pipeline is broadly coherent and each component is individually well-specified: precomputed multi-view embeddings, lexical sketches, and local graph artifacts are standard techniques, and the three scoring signals combine in a compatible way through a linear head. The optional RankNet-style query-delta refinement (finding Δq to match a listwise ordering) is the weakest point — it is described as "a quick RankNet-style step" but the optimization procedure (how many gradient steps, what loss exactly, convergence guarantees at inference time) is underspecified enough that an implementer would need to make non-trivial design choices. Additionally, the interaction between Bloom filter sketches and minhash for IDF-weighted overlap is slightly hand-wavy (Bloom filters support membership queries, not weighted intersection; minhash estimates Jaccard, not IDF-weighted overlap), requiring the implementer to reconcile these into a concrete implementation. These are notable but not blocking gaps — the overall architecture is sound and the rest of the pipeline is implementable.

The method is well-structured and internally consistent across its stages. The Context Sketch construction, PRJ token extraction, pointwise scoring, and pairwise LLM comparison all operate on the already-retrieved top-N candidates, satisfying the core constraint of not re-encoding the corpus. The Bradley-Terry aggregation from pairwise preferences is a standard, well-defined procedure, and the tournament scheduling (noisy quicksort/Swiss-system) is a known technique compatible with O(M log M) comparisons. The main minor gaps are: the PRJ extraction step (frequency + MI + contrastiveness weighting) is described at a high level and would require non-trivial implementation choices for the "light extractor," and the calibration of w1/w2 on held-out queries presupposes availability of such data without specifying how to handle cold-start. The optional refinement loop (recomputing PRJ weights and re-evaluating S0 near the decision boundary) is slightly underspecified in terms of convergence criteria, but it is framed as optional. None of these gaps are blocking — an ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable design choices.

The method is a well-structured, multi-stage pipeline where each component feeds naturally into the next. The steps are internally consistent: self-supervised pretraining → synthetic data generation → iterative self-training → few-shot fine-tuning follows a logical progression with no paradigm contradictions. The mechanisms are compatible (CTC/attention/transducer with shallow-fusion LM, adapter-based fine-tuning, WFST decoding are all established and combinable). Minor under-specifications exist — for instance, the domain-adversarial regularizer's discriminator architecture and training schedule are not detailed, and the exact confidence/entropy filtering thresholds require practitioner judgment — but none of these constitute blocking flaws. An experienced ML practitioner could implement this pipeline using existing toolkits (ESPnet, NeMo, fairseq) with reasonable design choices to fill the small gaps.

The method is broadly coherent and describes a plausible, implementable pipeline. The five stages connect logically: SSL/CTC pretraining → synthetic data generation with quality filtering → pseudo-labeling → curriculum with layer partitioning → inference-time LM/biasing. The mechanisms are compatible (XLS-R/WavLM with CTC and seq2seq heads is established practice; UniSpeech-style joint objectives are well-defined; shallow fusion and contextual biasing are standard). The main coherence weakness is in Step 4, where the layer partitioning scheme — simultaneously optimizing lower layers with CTC/contrastive SSL and upper layers with seq2seq cross-entropy — requires careful gradient routing and loss weighting that is described at a high level but not specified precisely enough for direct implementation (e.g., how gradients from the seq2seq decoder are prevented from flowing into lower layers, or how the CTC and contrastive losses are jointly backpropagated without interference). Additionally, the interaction between the "freeze or slow-learn" strategy in Step 1 and the "gradually unfreeze" strategy in Step 4 creates a mild tension that an implementer would need to reconcile. These are non-trivial design choices but not blocking contradictions — a competent ML practitioner could fill them in with reasonable decisions.

The pipeline is elaborate but each stage connects logically to the next: text mining → TTS synthesis → quality filtering via attribution → unified pretraining → self-training with round-trip filtering → fine-tuning with curriculum. The individual components (multilingual TTS, CTC pretraining, masked contrastive with VQ targets, self-training) are all established techniques that can be combined. The most notable coherence weakness is the "token-to-audio attribution scores via cross-attention perturbation" mechanism, which is described at a high level but is non-trivial to implement in a standard TTS codec model where cross-attention may not be straightforwardly interpretable or even present in the same form across architectures. Similarly, "aligning or initializing the ASR quantizer from the TTS codec" is plausible in principle but requires the two codebooks to operate in compatible acoustic spaces, which is an assumption that may not hold without significant additional engineering. These are real gaps an implementer would need to resolve, but they are not fundamental contradictions — they are under-specified mechanisms that a skilled ML practitioner could address with reasonable design choices.

The method is a well-structured pipeline combining established components (adapter/LoRA fine-tuning, TTS-based data augmentation, EMA teacher self-training, LM shallow fusion, curriculum learning) that are individually well-understood and mutually compatible. The integration logic is clearly described: synthetic data bootstraps early training, self-training with uncertainty filtering exploits unlabeled audio, and error-driven augmentation closes coverage gaps. The main coherence weakness is that several sub-components are under-specified in ways that would require non-trivial design choices: the "lightweight deterministic uncertainty head attached to the pre-logit/CTC space" is not precisely defined (how exactly uncertainty is computed deterministically from CTC logits vs. requiring MC dropout or ensembles), the "soft supervision target by distributing probability over top-k hypotheses" needs a concrete weighting scheme, and the interaction between the MBR/n-best risk loss and the CTC+attention objective requires careful implementation to avoid gradient conflicts. These are fillable gaps rather than blocking contradictions, and an experienced ML practitioner could resolve them with reasonable design choices.

The method is broadly coherent and well-structured, with each stage (visual pretraining, sim policy training, few-shot adaptation, online refinement) following logically from the previous. The components are individually well-established (contrastive cross-domain learning, teacher-student distillation, context-conditioned policies, meta-learning, conservative offline RL). However, there are a few notable gaps an implementer would need to resolve: the "coarse layout cues from structure-from-motion or scene clustering" for cross-domain positive pairing is under-specified and non-trivial to implement reliably across arbitrary deployment environments; the mechanism for the "safe probing sequence" is not defined (what actions, how safety is guaranteed, how long); and the interaction between the conservative offline RL regularizer and the self-supervised losses during adaptation lacks a concrete specification of how these objectives are balanced. The DAgger-lite risk critic is mentioned but its training procedure is not described. These are real gaps but none individually blocks implementation — a competent ML practitioner could make reasonable design choices to fill them without fundamentally redesigning the approach.

The pipeline is broadly coherent and each phase connects logically to the next. The main coherence tension is in Phase 3, where the method simultaneously proposes fine-tuning with mixed replay (a supervised/offline RL approach) and meta-RL with inner-loop gradient steps — these are distinct paradigms that require different training setups (meta-RL must be baked in from the start of pretraining, not added at fine-tuning time). The method states "initializing the policy with meta-RL" as if meta-RL is a deployment-time add-on, but MAML-style meta-RL requires the policy to have been trained with the meta-objective from the beginning, creating a prerequisite that is not fully addressed in Phase 1 or 2. The last-inch corrective module is reasonable but the handoff mechanism (how the main policy yields control to the corrector at the "task bottleneck pose") is underspecified. These are notable gaps but not fatal contradictions — an experienced practitioner could resolve them with reasonable design choices.

The pipeline is broadly coherent and each stage connects logically to the next: privileged RL → distillation → cross-domain alignment → on-hardware meta-adaptation. The most notable coherence tension is in the cross-domain alignment step, where "reconstructing a paired sim segment by replaying measured states or re-planning in the digital twin" is under-specified — in practice, obtaining truly paired sim-real frames with matching visual content is non-trivial and the method glosses over how state correspondence is established precisely enough to make the feature alignment loss meaningful. A second mild tension is that Stage 1 applies meta-RL for dynamics adaptation while Stage 2 freezes or conservatively updates perception during on-hardware adaptation, which is reasonable but the interaction between the FiLM dynamics adaptor and the frozen visual backbone is not fully specified (e.g., what inputs the FiLM adaptor conditions on at deployment). These are gaps an implementer would need to fill but are not fundamental contradictions; the overall architecture is compatible and the components (MAML-style meta-RL, BC distillation, contrastive/consistency alignment, PPO fine-tuning) are all standard and combinable.

The method combines many sophisticated components — Neural CDEs, VIB-style uncertainty, adversarial domain alignment, split latents, visual prompts, DAgger with pseudo-demonstrators, and optional off-policy RL — each of which is individually known but whose interactions introduce coherence concerns. The most serious issue is the interaction between the frozen CDE dynamics (step 4 says "keeping the shared latent and CDE dynamics largely fixed") and the visual adapter updates: the CDE's latent state is initialized by the image encoder, so if the adapter changes the image embedding distribution during real-world adaptation, the CDE dynamics trained on sim embeddings may receive out-of-distribution inputs, potentially destabilizing the continuous-time integration in ways not addressed. Additionally, the uncertainty signal is derived from a VIB-style latent rate on z_shared, but z_shared is supposed to remain largely fixed during fine-tuning, creating a tension between using it as a reliable uncertainty estimator and keeping it frozen. The pseudo-demonstrator (visual servoing/MPC) providing corrective labels is reasonable in principle but its compatibility with the CDE's continuous-time action decoding is underspecified — how discrete correction segments from a heuristic controller are integrated into the adjoint-trained CDE training loop is not explained. These are notable but not fully blocking gaps; an experienced ML practitioner could make reasonable design choices to bridge them.

The method is ambitious but largely coherent — each component (VQ codebook, typed modules, neural parser, structural EM, Gumbel-Softmax to REINFORCE transition, split-merge library management, compositionality losses) is a known technique and the connections between them are described with enough specificity that an ML practitioner could implement them. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the transition from differentiable relaxation to hard discrete sampling mid-training is described at a high level but the switching criterion and interaction with the EM loop are underspecified, creating a potential instability that would require non-trivial design choices; (2) the typed variable-binding mechanism with "named slots" and "typed latent carriers" is invoked as if straightforward but the concrete implementation of type checking during program tree construction and execution is not fully specified; (3) the interaction between the structural EM beam search and the gradient-based module updates creates a complex optimization landscape whose stability is assumed but not addressed. These are notable gaps but not fundamental contradictions — a skilled practitioner could fill them with reasonable design choices without redesigning the core approach.

The method is elaborate but internally consistent — each stage (DSL definition, teacher synthesis, draft-bind-execute, student SFT + process preference learning, backward-chaining inference, macro-operator abstraction) connects to the next with compatible mechanisms. The typed DSL with schema-constrained decoding is a well-understood technique, the teacher-student distillation pipeline is standard, and the backward-chaining planner with a learned value model is coherent. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the "soft world model" that simulates step effects and returns UNKNOWN is invoked but not specified enough for an implementer to build without significant design choices — it is a non-trivial component whose interface with the DSL executor is underspecified; (2) the "dynamic, state-dependent value margin" for process preference learning is described at a high level but the exact computation (how margin scales with state quality, how partial program states are featurized) is left vague; (3) the information-gain trigger for macro-operator abstraction is mentioned but the mechanism for learning typed signatures and summaries for new operators is not detailed. These are notable gaps but none individually blocks implementation — a competent ML practitioner could make reasonable design choices to fill them without redesigning the overall pipeline.

The method is ambitious and detailed, combining VQ-VAE-style slot extraction, CLIP-style grounding, Prolog-style symbolic execution, and template induction into a single pipeline. The individual components are each well-established in the literature, and their combination is described with enough specificity that an ML practitioner could identify what to build. However, there are notable coherence tensions: the interface between the differentiable VQ/slot-extraction front-end and the discrete Prolog-style depth-first search with backtracking is underspecified — specifically, how gradients flow through symbolic execution back to the Atomizer is not explained (the "program-trace consistency" loss is named but the mechanism for making this differentiable or using REINFORCE/straight-through is absent). Additionally, the "soft-logic regularizers cast as differentiable potentials" that are meant to stabilize the Prolog execution are described only at a high level, leaving a non-trivial design gap. These are real implementation challenges that would require significant redesign choices, but they do not make the overall pipeline incoherent — they are gaps an experienced practitioner could fill with known techniques (e.g., straight-through estimators, REINFORCE, or neural theorem provers).

The method is broadly coherent and combines recognizable components (VQ-style codebooks, straight-through Gumbel estimators, prototype layers, curriculum training, uncertainty-guided library growth) in compatible ways. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the "atom-graph" planner with pseudo-labels derived from "top-k retrieval over the atom-graph" creates a circular dependency — the graph is built from co-usage statistics that themselves depend on the planner's outputs, and the initialization/bootstrapping of this graph is not specified; (2) the two-stage "evidence→plan" procedure within the planner is described at a high level without specifying the architecture or how evidence is represented and converted to a DAG, leaving a non-trivial design gap; (3) the interaction between the shared and domain-specific prototype banks during gradient flow and the exact gating mechanism for domain indicators is underspecified. These are notable gaps an implementer would need to fill in, but none are fundamental contradictions — the overall pipeline is plausible and the components are individually well-understood.

HSGL is an ambitious and detailed hierarchical RL method that combines many well-established components (world models, change-point detection, graph centrality, HER-style relabeling, options framework, abstract MDPs, counterfactual attribution). The individual components are each technically grounded and their interfaces are mostly specified. However, there are notable coherence tensions: the counterfactual Shapley-style return decomposition requires simulating alternative trajectories by swapping subgoals mid-sequence using the learned world model, but the method does not specify how to condition the world model on a counterfactual subgoal choice while keeping the rest of the trajectory consistent — this is a non-trivial implementation challenge that is glossed over. Additionally, the joint training loop involves many interdependent components (subgoal detectors, skill policies, termination conditions, abstract MDP, meta-controller, world model) being updated simultaneously with complex feedback loops, and the method underspecifies how to stabilize this joint optimization (e.g., ordering of updates, frozen vs. live components). These are real gaps an implementer would need to resolve through significant design choices, but they do not fundamentally contradict each other or make the method unimplementable in principle.

The method combines many components that are individually known (LTL-to-automaton compilation, options framework, SVGD ensembles, Lagrange multipliers, hierarchical Bayes) but their integration introduces several under-specified interfaces. The transition-template clustering step — estimating next-state distributions after "light exploration" and grouping by similarity — is vague enough that an implementer would need to make substantial design choices (what similarity metric, what clustering algorithm, how to handle continuous state spaces, how cluster boundaries map to initiation sets). The "state-conditional candidate set" for action discretization in continuous control is described without specifying how candidates are generated or updated, which is a non-trivial design gap. The coordination mechanism requires a "shared embedding" between low-level progress and planner subgoals, but how this embedding is constructed or jointly trained is not explained. The SVGD planner ensemble, KL-regularized RL, hierarchical Bayesian prior, and Lagrange multiplier adaptation are all layered on top of each other without specifying how they interact during training (e.g., does SVGD operate over the KL-regularized objective, and how does the Lagrange multiplier interact with the KL term?). These are not fatal contradictions but represent multiple notable gaps that would require significant redesign effort beyond reasonable practitioner judgment calls.

HiLMC is an ambitious but broadly coherent hierarchical RL framework. The individual components (HER-based skill learning, GNN effect models, A*/beam search over skills, progress state Z, multi-resolution auxiliary heads, curriculum) are each well-established or clearly specified enough to implement. The interactions between components are described with sufficient detail: the state-writer is supervised by observed transitions, Q^H uses TD over executed skills, and the multi-depth heads share end-task returns. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the state-writer for Z is described as a learned module but its architecture and training signal are only loosely specified — "updates Z after each executed skill using (s, Z, i, g)" leaves the representational format of Z and how preconditions are "queryable" underspecified, requiring non-trivial design choices; (2) the interaction between the planner's look-ahead (which uses T_i^coarse to simulate outcomes) and the online refinement of those same models creates a moving-target issue that is acknowledged but not resolved; (3) the multi-resolution auxiliary heads V_k are attached at "every plan depth k" but how these depths map to actual timesteps during off-policy replay is not fully specified. These are notable gaps but not fundamental contradictions — an experienced ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable design choices without redesigning the overall approach.

The method is broadly coherent and combines well-established components (options framework, information bottleneck, graph neural networks, Bayesian belief tracking, shaped rewards) in a compatible way. The most notable coherence weakness is the "pseudo-interactive demonstrator" in step 3, which is described as having access to the learned subgoal graph and a planning heuristic to propose corrective labels during rollouts — but the mechanism by which this demonstrator intervenes (online relabeling? DAgger-style querying? offline relabeling in replay?) is underspecified enough that an implementer would need to make non-trivial design choices. Additionally, the interaction between the group-sparsity pruning of options and the periodic re-mining of subgoals (step 4) could create instability that is acknowledged but not resolved. These are notable gaps but not blocking contradictions; the overall pipeline is internally consistent and a skilled ML practitioner could fill in the missing pieces with reasonable choices.

The overall PCIC framework is internally consistent — Proposer/Verifier/Executor roles map cleanly onto each domain instantiation, and the multi-term training objective is well-specified. However, several notable gaps would require an implementer to make significant design choices: the joint training of Proposer, Verifier, and Executor with interdependent losses raises chicken-and-egg bootstrapping issues (the Verifier needs good scratchpads to train, but the Proposer needs a trained Verifier to improve), and no concrete initialization or curriculum is specified. In the vision instantiation, the "differentiable geometric consistency checks" and "learned stopper" are described at a high level but lack sufficient detail on architecture or how gradients flow through discrete token scratchpads. The RL instantiation's world-model Verifier using "short rollouts or Q-estimates" is plausible but the interaction between sketch revision and the receding-horizon commitment policy is underspecified. These are real gaps but not fundamental contradictions — a skilled ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable design choices.

The method is ambitious and broadly coherent in its high-level framing — a shared scratchpad/policy/verifier loop instantiated across three domains. However, several components introduce notable coherence tensions. The per-step reward r_t = softplus(G(s_t) − G(s_{t+1})) is defined as a difference in progress metrics between consecutive states, but G(s_{t+1}) depends on the next state, which requires either a learned value estimator or lookahead — this is not fully specified and conflates a reward signal with a value function in a way that would require non-trivial design choices to implement. The simultaneous use of group-normalized advantages with KL, a DPO objective with dynamic value margin, and an information-gain regularizer creates a multi-objective training setup whose interaction and relative weighting are left unspecified, making stable training non-trivial to achieve. The twisted SMC inference procedure is technically sound in principle but its integration with domain-specific verifiers and the IG-based resampling weights is described at a level of abstraction that would require substantial design work to operationalize consistently across all three domains. These are not fatal contradictions but represent meaningful gaps an implementer would need to fill through redesign rather than straightforward engineering choices.

The ICP framework is broadly coherent in its high-level design — the decompose/gather/verify/commit loop is a recognizable and consistent abstraction, and each domain instantiation maps the operators to plausible domain-specific mechanisms. However, several notable gaps appear across instantiations. The cold-start teacher trajectory synthesis is underspecified: for RL, generating "expert sub-trajectories that alternate reasoning and external actions" with explicit state sketches requires a teacher that already performs ICP-style reasoning, which is circular unless a separate oracle is assumed. The faithfulness regularizer is described conceptually but its operationalization across domains (how agreement between "executed checks" and "narrated steps" is measured for a vision model vs. a reranker) is left vague enough that an implementer would need to make substantial design choices. The recommendation instantiation's "ordering-only feedback updates the retriever's query embedding" mechanism is underspecified — the gradient path from listwise reranker outputs back to retriever embeddings at inference time is not explained. These are real gaps but not fundamental contradictions; a skilled ML practitioner could fill them with reasonable design choices without redesigning the core framework.

CoComp is a well-structured proposal with clearly articulated three-block decompositions across three domains, and the training recipe (multi-task losses, DAgger-style correction, uncertainty gating) is broadly compatible with each instantiation. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) the "pseudo-demonstrator" in the RL instantiation that generates subgoals over a "learned graph of reachable states" is under-specified — building and maintaining such a graph in continuous or high-dimensional state spaces is itself a substantial unsolved component that cannot be assumed available; (2) the uncertainty gate's training procedure (how it is jointly trained with the three blocks without collapsing to always-easy or always-hard routing) is not explained; and (3) the vision instantiation mixes a "tiny captioner" producing text with a downstream vision head, requiring a cross-modal fusion mechanism that is mentioned but not specified. These are notable gaps an implementer would need to fill by design, but none individually constitutes a fundamental contradiction — the overall paradigm is coherent and the domain instantiations are plausible extensions of known techniques.

HAMMER is a densely specified system with many interacting components, most of which are individually well-understood (local attention, ANN retrieval, cross-attention, contrastive objectives, REINFORCE gradients). The overall pipeline is coherent in its broad strokes: chunk processing → write controller → bounded memory → ANN retrieval → cross-attention reads. However, several mechanisms introduce non-trivial coherence tensions. The "utility predictor updated online from gradients and future loss reductions" is under-specified in a streaming setting where future loss is not yet observed at write time — the delayed label construction via gradient-attribution or influence estimates is mentioned but not concretely defined, leaving a critical training loop gap. The one-step lookahead queries derived from "predicted next-token features" require a sub-model to predict future hidden states, which is an additional component not described. The "retrospective compressor" that uses restricted attention across candidate spans without revisiting full history is plausible but its interaction with the already-written memory items (do summaries get updated in-place?) is unresolved. The O(sqrt N) or O(log N) memory bound claim through "budget scheduling" is asserted without a concrete scheduling rule. These are real gaps an implementer would need to design around, but none individually blocks the overall architecture from being built in a reasonable approximation.

The method is broadly coherent and combines well-established components (RWKV-style recurrence, token pruning, dual memory buffers, retrieval-augmented generation via soft prompts) in a compatible pipeline. The most notable coherence weakness is the MaxLoss-style marginal utility scoring during the consolidation step: computing "increase in validation loss when a slot is withheld" requires repeated forward passes per candidate slot at every consolidation step, which is computationally expensive and the method does not specify how this is approximated or made tractable within the stated compute budget. Additionally, the joint training objective across three interacting components (pruning/compression, memory selection policy, and read-time routing) is described at a high level without specifying how gradients flow through the discrete token pruning and slot selection operations, leaving implementers to resolve non-trivial differentiability issues. These are real gaps but not fundamental contradictions — an experienced ML practitioner could fill them with standard approximations (e.g., straight-through estimators, influence-function approximations to MaxLoss) without redesigning the overall architecture.

The method is broadly coherent and combines well-established components (deformable attention, ANN retrieval, slot attention, EMA updates, gradient-based attribution) in a compatible way. The main coherence weaknesses are: (1) gradient-based attribution for online core-token selection during inference is computationally expensive and requires a defined objective/prompt at ingestion time, which may not always be available in a streaming setting — this is a notable practical gap; (2) the routing mechanism that decides the budgeted mix across tiers is described only at a high level ("routes the query") without specifying how this router is trained or parameterized, leaving an implementer to design this component from scratch; (3) the interaction between the symbolic world-state writer (which extracts normalized facts) and the neural backbone is underspecified — it's unclear whether this is a separate NLP module, a trained head, or a rule-based system. These gaps are real but not individually fatal; an ML practitioner could make reasonable design choices to fill them without fundamentally redesigning the approach.

STREAM is a well-articulated system combining several established components (streaming latent states, external memory, dual-scale attention, contrastive retrieval) in a broadly compatible way. The overall architecture is coherent and an ML practitioner could implement most pieces. However, there are notable under-specifications: the "continuous-time latent state" mechanism is described only abstractly — it is unclear whether this is an ODE-based state, an RNN, or a gated update, and how it interfaces with the discrete event writes and the backbone transformer is not specified. The pseudo-relevance feedback refinement step is mentioned but not defined (what form does the re-ranking take, and how does it interact with the contrastive training objective?). The auxiliary reconstruction loss coupling the streaming latent to discrete event summaries is conceptually reasonable but the gradient flow between the continuous latent and the discrete memory index is not explained. These gaps are fillable by a practitioner with reasonable design choices but would require non-trivial decisions not dictated by the method.

The method describes a well-established MoE-style architecture with hierarchical routing, two-phase training, and hardware-aware inference. All components are technically compatible: Gumbel-Softmax/straight-through for differentiable routing, load-balancing auxiliary losses, capacity factors, and kernel fusion are standard, co-existing techniques in the literature. The two-phase training (soft→hard routing annealing) is coherent with the inference-time hard top-k decisions. The uncertainty-aware fallback and default expert are consistent with the overflow handling described. Minor under-specifications exist — the "hierarchical router" structure is not precisely defined (e.g., how hierarchy is organized across layers vs. within a layer), and the interaction between the uncertainty-aware auxiliary head and the routing scores is loosely described — but these are design choices an ML practitioner could fill in without fundamental redesign.

The overall framework is coherent and draws on well-established components (Straight-Through Gumbel-Softmax, KL distillation, top-k gating, sparse matmuls). The most serious coherence weakness is the "twist" head mechanism: the description says it is "learned from the improvement in next-layer loss when toggling candidate subsets" at training time, but this requires evaluating counterfactual forward passes (toggling subsets) during training, which is computationally expensive and not straightforwardly differentiable — the exact training procedure for the twist head is underspecified enough that an implementer would need to redesign this component. Additionally, the interaction between the twist-modulated thresholds and the global FLOP budget constraint is not fully specified (how dynamic threshold relaxation/tightening is reconciled with a hard global budget). These are notable but not fully blocking gaps; the rest of the method (gate parameterization, KL compensation, weight layout, inference pipeline) is described with sufficient clarity to implement.

The method combines several well-established techniques (MoE-style branch routing, Matryoshka nested slicing, early exit, and salience caching) into a single framework. The individual components are each coherent, but their interaction creates notable under-specification. Most critically, the interaction between Matryoshka slice selection and branch routing is not fully specified — when the router selects "top-k branches with their smallest necessary slices," it is unclear how the slice size is determined per branch per input, and how the concat+projection merge handles variable-width branch outputs when different branches use different slice sizes.
